notes: Official party policy source inventory and manual structured rows. Add voter-facing policy rows only after manual review against the official source URL.

rows.headline: Immigration restriction and removals
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would freeze non-essential immigration, stop small boat crossings by leaving the ECHR, detain and deport illegal arrivals, restrict access to benefits, and introduce an employer immigration tax while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
rows.detailedSummary: Freeze non-essential immigration, with essential skills mainly around healthcare as the exception.|Leave the ECHR as part of its small boats plan and detain and deport illegal arrivals.|Bar student dependants and limit routes that Reform says abuse immigration rules.|Require five years of residency and employment before claiming benefits.|Raise employer National Insurance for foreign workers, while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a highly restrictive immigration position that links border control to wages, housing pressure and public-service capacity.

rows.headline: Tax cuts, waste reduction and business deregulation
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut wasteful spending, raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000, raise the higher-rate threshold to £70,000, cut energy taxes, reduce corporation tax, lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and reduce regulation on business.
rows.detailedSummary: Require departments to reduce wasteful spending without touching frontline services.|Raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000 and the higher-rate threshold to £70,000.|Cut residential stamp duty and abolish inheritance tax for estates under £2m.|Lift the minimum corporation tax profit threshold to £100,000 and reduce the main corporation tax rate.|Lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and abolish IR35 rules.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames economic policy around tax cuts, spending restraint and deregulation, with the fiscal balance dependent on claimed savings and growth effects.

rows.headline: NHS waiting lists and healthcare capacity
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says the NHS should remain free at the point of delivery while being reformed to reduce waiting lists. Its pledges include tax relief for frontline NHS and social care staff, ending training caps for UK medical students, using independent healthcare capacity and introducing vouchers where NHS waits exceed set limits.
rows.detailedSummary: Keep healthcare free at the point of delivery while reforming the NHS to improve outcomes and reduce waiting lists.|Give frontline NHS and social care staff zero basic-rate tax for three years.|End training caps for UK medical students and write off student fees over NHS service.|Use independent and not-for-profit healthcare capacity in the UK and overseas.|Offer NHS patients a voucher for private treatment where GP, consultant or operation waits exceed stated limits.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps a free-at-use NHS commitment but relies heavily on staffing tax incentives, management reform and independent-sector capacity.

rows.headline: Housebuilding, planning and local priority
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would increase home ownership by accelerating housebuilding, cutting immigration, fast-tracking brownfield development, changing social housing priority rules, restoring landlord finance-cost relief and using construction technology to speed up building.
rows.detailedSummary: Unleash housebuilding and cut immigration as part of its housing approach.|Fast-track planning and tax incentives for brownfield development.|Prioritise local people and those who have paid into the system in social housing law.|Restore landlords' rights to deduct finance costs and mortgage interest from rental income tax.|Use modular construction and digital technology to speed up building and reduce waste.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines supply-side planning changes with a strong local-priority frame and a direct link between housing pressure and immigration.

rows.headline: Curriculum, discipline and higher education reform
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says education should build skills, character and pride in Britain. Its pledges include a patriotic curriculum, banning transgender ideology in schools, tax relief on independent education, scrapping interest on student loans, more pupil referral units and requiring universities to offer two-year undergraduate courses.
rows.detailedSummary: Introduce a patriotic curriculum in primary and secondary schools.|Ban transgender ideology in primary and secondary schools and require single-sex facilities.|Apply 20% tax relief on independent education.|Scrap interest on student loans and extend repayment periods.|Double pupil referral units for violent and disruptive students.|Require universities to provide two-year undergraduate courses.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames education through cultural curriculum change, school discipline and reduced higher-education costs rather than a broad funding expansion.

rows.headline: Net zero rollback and domestic energy
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says net zero is raising bills and weakening industry. It would scrap net zero and related subsidies, tax renewable subsidies, fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences, test shale gas sites and pursue nuclear, lithium, gas, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal options.
rows.detailedSummary: Scrap net zero and related subsidies.|Apply equivalent taxes to annual renewable energy subsidies.|Fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences.|Grant shale gas licences on test sites for two years, with local compensation schemes if production follows.|Fast-track small modular nuclear reactors and explore UK lithium, gas turbines, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This prioritises lower energy bills and domestic supply over the current net-zero timetable, while retaining some technology-led environmental measures.

rows.headline: Benefits conditionality and back-to-work reform
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says those who can work should work. Its benefits pledges include raising the income tax starting point to strengthen work incentives, reforming benefit support and training, applying a two-strike rule for job offers, and using face-to-face independent medical assessments for disability and work capability payments.
rows.detailedSummary: Raise the income tax starting point to £20,000 to make work pay and incentivise people on benefits into work.|Reform benefit support and training to help people back into work, with particular focus on 16-34 year olds.|Apply a two-strike rule where jobseekers and those fit to work must find employment within four months or accept a job after two offers, otherwise benefits are withdrawn.|Use face-to-face Personal Independence Payment and Work Capability Assessment checks.|Require independent medical assessments to prove eligibility, while exempting people with severe disabilities or serious long-term illnesses from regular checks.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames welfare mainly through work incentives, conditionality and assessment reform rather than through higher benefit entitlements.

rows.headline: Roads, HS2 and critical infrastructure
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would scrap the remaining HS2 project, ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods, remove net-zero vehicle mandates, improve existing rail and road links, and create a new ownership model for utilities with 50% public ownership and 50% UK pension fund ownership.
rows.detailedSummary: Scrap the remaining HS2 project.|Legislate to ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods.|Remove bans on petrol and diesel cars and legal requirements for manufacturers to sell electric cars as part of scrapping net zero policy.|Improve existing rail and road links, focusing on coastal regions, Wales, the North and the Midlands.|Create a new critical national infrastructure model with 50% public ownership and 50% ownership by UK pension funds.|Merge and overhaul infrastructure funding bodies and remove net-zero objectives from the infrastructure funding process.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines a pro-motorist transport position with a more interventionist approach to utilities and critical infrastructure ownership.

rows.headline: Constitutional reform and sovereignty
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would leave the European Convention on Human Rights, reform the Human Rights Act, replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber, reform the Civil Service, restrict postal voting, support a referendum on proportional representation, and introduce a British Bill of Rights.
rows.detailedSummary: Leave the European Convention on Human Rights and reform the Human Rights Act to put the rights of law-abiding people first.|Replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber.|End political appointees in the Civil Service and replace Civil Service leaders with successful professionals from the private sector as political appointees.|Restrict postal voting to elderly, disabled and housebound voters.|Support a referendum on proportional representation for the House of Commons.|Introduce a British Bill of Rights covering freedoms, privacy, surveillance and public accountability.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This puts sovereignty and institutional change at the centre, combining rights-law withdrawal with proposals for parliamentary, voting and civil-service reform.

rows.headline: Defence, Brexit sovereignty and foreign aid
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut foreign aid by 50%, scrap retained EU regulations, abandon the Windsor Framework, resist EU defence integration, prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement, and increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years.
rows.detailedSummary: Cut foreign aid by 50% and review the effectiveness of overseas aid and global bodies funded by the UK.|Scrap retained EU regulations with immediate effect.|Abandon the Windsor Framework, which Reform says leaves Northern Ireland too closely tied to EU rules.|Resist EU defence integration and protect independence for Britain's armed forces.|Prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement.|Increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years, with an urgent armed forces pay review and a dedicated ministerial department for veterans.
rows.sourceTitle: Reform UK Contract with You 2024
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames foreign policy through sovereignty, reduced aid spending, Brexit renegotiation and higher defence capacity rather than broader international-development commitments.

rows.headline: Secure borders and managed migration
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will restore order and control to the immigration and asylum system, reduce net migration, create a Border Security Command to target smuggling gangs, reform points-based migration rules, link immigration with skills policy, and increase returns for people with no right to stay.
rows.detailedSummary: Create a new Border Security Command with investigators, intelligence officers and cross-border police officers.|Seek new security cooperation with the EU to support joint investigations against smuggling gangs.|Clear the asylum backlog, end asylum hotels and restore a system that operates swiftly, firmly and fairly.|Set up a returns and enforcement unit with additional staff and negotiate more returns arrangements.|Reform the points-based immigration system and link migration routes to skills and workforce plans.|Reduce reliance on overseas workers in sectors such as health, social care and construction by requiring domestic training plans.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Strong foundations
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines tougher border enforcement with a skills-and-workforce approach to legal migration, rather than a blanket freeze.

rows.headline: Stability, investment and industrial strategy
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will pursue economic growth through fiscal stability, an industrial strategy, a statutory Industrial Strategy Council, a National Wealth Fund, planning reform, business-tax certainty and stronger partnership with business, trade unions, local leaders and devolved governments.
rows.detailedSummary: Use fiscal rules requiring day-to-day costs to be met by revenues and debt falling as a share of the economy by the fifth year of the forecast.|Introduce a new industrial strategy and establish an Industrial Strategy Council on a statutory footing.|Create a National Wealth Fund capitalised with £7.3bn to support growth and clean energy missions.|Cap corporation tax at 25% for the parliament and publish a business taxation roadmap.|Retain permanent full expensing and the annual investment allowance for small business.|Use public investment to unlock private investment, create jobs and support returns for taxpayers.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Kickstart economic growth
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a growth strategy built around stability, planning reform and state-backed investment rather than large headline tax cuts.

rows.headline: Waiting lists, prevention and NHS reform
rows.shortSummary: Labour says the NHS will remain publicly owned and publicly funded while being reformed to cut waiting times, deliver 40,000 more appointments each week, double CT and MRI scanners, use spare independent-sector capacity, strengthen workforce planning, and shift care toward prevention and community services.
rows.detailedSummary: Keep the NHS publicly owned and publicly funded.|Deliver an extra two million NHS operations, scans and appointments each year, equivalent to 40,000 more appointments each week.|Use shared waiting lists and spare independent-sector capacity to diagnose and treat patients more quickly.|Publish regular independent workforce planning across health and social care and deliver the NHS long-term workforce plan.|Introduce a Fit for the Future fund to double CT and MRI scanners.|Shift the NHS toward prevention, earlier diagnosis, community care and stronger mental health support.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Build an NHS fit for the future
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps the public NHS model while using reform, workforce planning and limited spare independent capacity to reduce waiting lists.

rows.headline: Housebuilding, renters and leasehold reform
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament, reform planning, restore mandatory housing targets, prioritise brownfield development, use grey belt land under golden rules, build new towns, abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions, strengthen renters' rights, and reform leasehold.
rows.detailedSummary: Build 1.5 million new homes over the parliament.|Update the National Planning Policy Framework and restore mandatory housing targets.|Fund additional planning officers and require up-to-date Local Plans.|Take a brownfield-first approach while using lower-quality grey belt land under golden rules.|Build a new generation of new towns and introduce cross-boundary strategic planning.|Abolish Section 21 no-fault evictions and empower renters to challenge unreasonable rent increases.|Act on leasehold reform, commonhold and unfair maintenance costs.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Kickstart economic growth
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines a supply-side housebuilding target with stronger renter and leaseholder protections.

rows.headline: Teachers, curriculum and skills reform
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, open more than 3,000 primary school-based nurseries, provide free breakfast clubs in every primary school, modernise curriculum and assessment, reform inspection, create Skills England, and replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a Growth and Skills Levy.
rows.detailedSummary: Recruit 6,500 new expert teachers, especially in shortage subjects and hard-to-recruit areas.|Open more than 3,000 nurseries by upgrading space in primary schools.|Fund free breakfast clubs in every primary school.|Review curriculum and assessment to create a broad, inclusive and innovative curriculum.|Replace single headline Ofsted grades with report cards and bring multi-academy trusts into inspection.|Create Skills England and transform further education colleges into Technical Excellence Colleges.|Replace the Apprenticeships Levy with a flexible Growth and Skills Levy.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This focuses on teacher supply, early years, school standards and skills pathways rather than a single structural school reform.

rows.headline: Child poverty, work support and social security review
rows.shortSummary: Labour says good work will be central to tackling poverty and inequality. Its pledges include reviewing Universal Credit so it makes work pay and tackles poverty, creating more good jobs, reforming employment support, supporting disabled people and people with health conditions into work, and developing a strategy to reduce child poverty.
rows.detailedSummary: Review Universal Credit so that it makes work pay and tackles poverty.|Develop an ambitious strategy to reduce child poverty.|Introduce free breakfast clubs in every primary school as an initial anti-poverty step.|Bring Jobcentre Plus and the National Careers Service together into a national jobs and careers service.|Devolve funding so local areas can shape joined-up work, health and skills support.|Support more disabled people and people with health conditions into work while reforming or replacing the Work Capability Assessment.|Establish a youth guarantee of training, an apprenticeship or support to find work for all 18- to 21-year-olds.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Break down barriers to opportunity
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames welfare through child poverty, employment support and work incentives, with Universal Credit review rather than a fully specified replacement system.

rows.headline: Clean power and Great British Energy
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will make Britain a clean energy superpower through clean power by 2030, Great British Energy, a Green Prosperity Plan, more onshore wind, solar and offshore wind, investment in carbon capture, hydrogen and marine energy, no new oil and gas exploration licences, and a permanent ban on fracking.
rows.detailedSummary: Work with the private sector to double onshore wind, triple solar power and quadruple offshore wind by 2030.|Create Great British Energy as a publicly owned company capitalised with £8.3bn.|Invest through the Green Prosperity Plan and National Wealth Fund to support clean-energy jobs and industries.|Invest in carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, marine energy and long-term energy storage.|Maintain a strategic reserve of gas power stations for security of supply.|Do not issue new licences to explore new oil and gas fields, do not grant new coal licences, and ban fracking for good.|Use a Local Power Plan for distributed clean-power projects with communities and local authorities.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Make Britain a clean energy superpower
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This treats climate policy as an industrial and energy-security mission, with public investment and a faster clean-power timetable.

rows.headline: Public rail, roads and local transport powers
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will modernise transport by fixing an additional one million potholes each year, supporting electric vehicle charging and a 2030 phase-out date for new petrol and diesel cars, bringing rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or fail, creating Great British Railways, and giving local leaders stronger bus and transport powers.
rows.detailedSummary: Fix an additional one million potholes across England in each year of the parliament.|Support the transition to electric vehicles by accelerating charge-point rollout and restoring the 2030 phase-out date for new internal-combustion cars.|Bring rail passenger services into public ownership as contracts expire or are broken through failure to deliver, without taxpayer compensation.|Create Great British Railways as a unified system focused on reliable, affordable and efficient services.|Create a new passenger watchdog to drive up rail standards.|Give local leaders powers to franchise local bus services and lift the ban on municipal ownership.|Give mayors powers to create unified and integrated transport systems and promote active travel networks.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Kickstart economic growth
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines public rail reform and stronger local transport powers with continued support for roads and electric-vehicle transition.

rows.headline: Standards, Lords reform and votes at 16
rows.shortSummary: Labour says it will restore public service in politics through an Ethics and Integrity Commission, stronger post-government employment rules, a House of Commons Modernisation Committee, an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles, House of Lords reform, improved voter registration, votes at 16 and stronger political donation rules.
rows.detailedSummary: Establish a new independent Ethics and Integrity Commission.|Review and update post-government employment rules, including stronger sanctions for abuses.|Give the Independent Adviser on Ministerial Interests powers to start investigations.|Create a House of Commons Modernisation Committee to reform procedures and standards.|Support an immediate ban on MPs taking paid advisory or consultancy roles.|Remove hereditary peers from the House of Lords, introduce a mandatory retirement age and consult on replacing the Lords with a more representative second chamber.|Improve voter registration, address voter ID inconsistencies, give 16- and 17-year-olds the vote in all elections and strengthen donation rules.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Serving the country
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This focuses on political standards, parliamentary reform and widening democratic participation rather than proportional representation.

rows.headline: NATO, Europe and international law
rows.shortSummary: Labour says Britain should reconnect with allies while staying outside the EU. Its pledges include an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the nuclear deterrent, a Strategic Defence Review, a path to 2.5% of GDP defence spending, an improved UK-EU relationship without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement, stronger European security cooperation, continued Ukraine support, and commitment to international law and the ECHR.
rows.detailedSummary: Maintain an unshakeable commitment to NATO and the UK nuclear deterrent.|Conduct a Strategic Defence Review and set out a path to spending 2.5% of GDP on defence.|Stay outside the EU while seeking to reset and deepen ties with European partners without returning to the single market, customs union or freedom of movement.|Seek an ambitious UK-EU security pact and closer defence and security cooperation with key European allies.|Remain a member of the European Convention on Human Rights.|Maintain military, financial, diplomatic and political support for Ukraine.|Bring forward a defence industrial strategy and strengthen support for armed forces communities and veterans.
rows.sourceTitle: Labour manifesto: Britain reconnected
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a pro-NATO and pro-European-cooperation position that keeps Brexit institutional red lines while seeking closer practical ties.

rows.headline: Legal migration cap and Rwanda removals
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would reduce migration through a binding legal cap on work and family visas, annual parliamentary votes on the cap, higher visa fees, tighter Skilled Worker and family income thresholds, restricted visas for countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities, and monthly Rwanda removal flights for illegal migrants.
rows.detailedSummary: Introduce a binding legal cap on work and family visas, with the cap falling every year of the parliament and requiring an annual parliamentary vote.|Raise Skilled Worker and family income thresholds and increase all visa fees, while removing the student discount from the Immigration Health Surcharge.|Restrict visa access from countries that do not cooperate on UK priorities such as illegal migration.|Continue safe and legal routes for people fleeing Hong Kong, Ukraine and Afghanistan, but give Parliament control of a capped number of places.|Run regular monthly Rwanda flights for illegal migrants until small boat crossings are stopped.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a restrictive immigration position built around legally binding numerical control, higher route thresholds and deterrence-based removals policy.

rows.headline: Tax cuts, fiscal rules and business investment
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027, abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament, introduce a Triple Lock Plus for pensioners, keep debt falling and borrowing below fiscal-rule limits, and support business investment, R&D, trade and small firms.
rows.detailedSummary: Cut employee National Insurance by another 2p by April 2027 as part of a long-term ambition to end the double tax on work when conditions allow.|Abolish the main rate of self-employed National Insurance by the end of the parliament.|Introduce a Triple Lock Plus so the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.|Continue fiscal rules requiring public sector net debt to fall and public sector net borrowing to stay below 3% of GDP in the fifth year of the forecast.|Raise at least a further £6bn a year from tackling tax avoidance and evasion by the end of the parliament.|Support SMEs, R&D, trade deals and business investment as part of a private-sector growth model.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines headline tax cuts with fiscal-rule discipline, relying on claimed savings, tax-compliance revenue and private-sector growth.

rows.headline: NHS staffing, facilities and productivity
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would increase NHS spending above inflation each year, deliver 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023, continue 40 new hospitals by 2030, expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP and diagnostic services, make the NHS App the single front door for services, and provide a multi-year settlement for social care.
rows.detailedSummary: Increase NHS spending above inflation in each year of the parliament.|Back the NHS Long Term Workforce Plan, with 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors than in 2023 by the end of the parliament.|Continue to deliver 40 new hospitals by 2030 and invest proportionately more in out-of-hospital services over time.|Expand Pharmacy First, modernise GP surgeries and increase Community Diagnostic Centres to move care closer to people's homes.|Make the NHS App the single front door for NHS services.|Give local authorities a multi-year funding settlement for social care and implement planned reforms to cap social care costs from October 2025.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps the existing NHS model while stressing workforce expansion, digital access, community diagnosis and productivity improvements.

rows.headline: 1.6 million homes and home ownership
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would deliver 1.6 million homes in England, focus on brownfield development and planning changes, make the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 permanent, launch a new Help to Buy equity loan, complete leasehold reform, pass a Renters Reform Bill, and continue support for leaseholders affected by historic building safety problems.
rows.detailedSummary: Deliver 1.6 million homes in England in the next parliament.|Abolish legacy EU nutrient neutrality rules and provide a fast-track planning route for new homes on previously developed land in the 20 largest cities.|Make permanent the first-time buyer Stamp Duty threshold of £425,000 and launch a new Help to Buy equity loan of up to 20% for new-build homes.|Keep people's homes protected from Capital Gains Tax and avoid increasing Stamp Duty rates or levels.|Complete leasehold reform by capping ground rents, moving them toward peppercorn over time, ending misuse of forfeiture and making commonhold easier.|Pass a Renters Reform Bill with court reforms needed to fully abolish Section 21 and strengthen grounds for landlords.|Continue developer-funded remediation programmes for mid- and high-rise buildings affected by historic building safety problems.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a housing offer centred on ownership, brownfield-led supply and targeted renter and leaseholder reform rather than rent controls.

rows.headline: Apprenticeships, schools and National Service
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil, create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England each year by the end of the parliament by closing university courses with the worst outcomes, introduce the Advanced British Standard for 16-19 education, and establish compulsory National Service for 18-year-olds.
rows.detailedSummary: Protect day-to-day schools spending in real terms per pupil.|Create 100,000 more apprenticeships in England every year by the end of the parliament.|Fund extra apprenticeships by changing the law to close university courses in England with the worst outcomes for students.|Transform 16-19 education through the Advanced British Standard, with a broader education and a reduced divide between academic and technical learning.|Introduce compulsory National Service for every 18-year-old, with a choice between a competitive military placement and civic service roles.|Establish a Royal Commission, funding rising to £2.5bn in the final year of the parliament and a new National Service Act for the modern National Service model.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames education through skills, apprenticeships and civic service, with university-course restrictions used to fund the apprenticeship expansion.

rows.headline: Work-first welfare and pensions protection
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would protect pensioners through the Triple Lock Plus while tightening welfare-to-work policy. Their pledges include changing sickness-benefit assessments, improving PIP assessments, overhauling fit notes, testing integration with WorkWell, and keeping benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.
rows.detailedSummary: Introduce the Triple Lock Plus so both the State Pension and pensioner tax-free allowance rise with the highest of inflation, earnings or 2.5%.|Improve PIP assessments to provide a more objective consideration of needs and stop claims rising unsustainably.|Tighten how the benefits system assesses capability for work, including changes from September 2025 for people with more moderate mental health issues or mobility problems who may be able to engage with work.|Overhaul fit notes so people are not signed off sick as a default, moving responsibility toward specialist work and health professionals.|Test integrating fit-note reform with WorkWell to provide tailored support to help people stay in or return to work.|Frame benefits as a safety net rather than a lifestyle choice.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines pensioner protection with a stricter disability and sickness-benefit assessment agenda focused on work incentives.

rows.headline: Net zero by 2050 with lower levies and energy security
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they remain committed to net zero by 2050 but would take a pragmatic approach that lowers green levies on household bills, rules out new green levies and a frequent flyer levy, continues North Sea investment allowances, backs new gas power stations for reliability, expands renewables and nuclear, and keeps the fracking moratorium.
rows.detailedSummary: Remain committed to delivering net zero by 2050 through a pragmatic, proportionate and realistic approach.|Ensure annual policy costs and levies on household energy bills are lower in each year of the parliament than in 2023 and rule out further green levies.|Rule out road pricing schemes and a frequent flyer levy.|Maintain investment allowances for the North Sea and continue backing the North Sea Transition Deal.|Back up renewables with new gas power stations to maintain reliable energy when weather does not provide enough renewable power.|Treble offshore wind capacity and approve two new fleets of Small Modular Reactors within the first 100 days of the parliament.|Retain the current moratorium on fracking.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This accepts the 2050 net-zero target but prioritises household cost control, gas backup and domestic energy security.

rows.headline: Local roads, motorists and rail reform
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses funded by cancelling HS2 phase two, including £8.3bn for potholes and road resurfacing. They would back drivers by stopping road pricing, extend the £2 bus fare cap in England, maintain no new smart motorways, and create Great British Railways through a private-public partnership.
rows.detailedSummary: Invest £36bn in local roads, rail and buses to drive regional growth, funded by cancelling the second phase of HS2.|Use £8.3bn to fill potholes and resurface roads, with some funding brought forward.|Back drivers by stopping road pricing and continuing a Plan for Drivers approach on bus lanes, roadworks penalties and yellow box junction enforcement.|Extend the £2 bus fare cap in England for the whole parliament.|Maintain the pledge to build no new smart motorways and invest in improving existing smart motorway safety.|Introduce a Rail Reform Bill to create Great British Railways, headquartered in Derby, as a private-public partnership with reliable services, simpler tickets and more open-access competition.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This puts roads and motorists at the centre while retaining rail reform through a mixed public-private model rather than nationalisation.

rows.headline: First Past the Post, voter ID and constitutional continuity
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would maintain the UK's constitutional settlement, keep First Past the Post for elections, keep the voting age at 18, maintain voter ID rules to tackle voting fraud, legislate to clarify biological sex in the Equality Act, protect free speech and press freedom, and support greater accountability in devolved parliaments.
rows.detailedSummary: Remain committed to the First Past the Post system for elections, maintaining the direct link with the local voter.|Keep the voting age at 18.|Maintain rules to tackle voting fraud, including the requirement to show ID.|Introduce primary legislation to clarify that the protected characteristic of sex in the Equality Act means biological sex.|Defend freedom of speech and freedom of the press, including opposition to state regulation of the press and support for the Freedom of Speech Act in universities.|Support greater accountability within devolved parliaments and comparable data across the UK so public-service performance can be compared.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a continuity-focused constitutional position, resisting electoral-system and voting-age changes while tightening identity and rights-law definitions.

rows.headline: Defence, NATO, Ukraine and national interest
rows.shortSummary: The Conservatives say they would raise defence spending to 2.5% of GDP by 2030, campaign for NATO allies to adopt the same baseline, sustain support for Ukraine, strengthen defence industry and procurement, maintain AUKUS and other partnerships, keep the UK outward-looking on trade and security, and return development spending to 0.7% of GNI when fiscal circumstances allow.
rows.detailedSummary: Hit 2.5% of GDP on defence in 2030 and campaign for all NATO allies to set a 2.5% baseline by 2030.|Guarantee Ukraine the support it needs for the long haul, including current levels of support for as long as required and work to use immobilised Russian assets to support Ukraine.|Deliver a National Defence and Resilience Plan and invest in future military capabilities.|Strengthen defence industrial partnerships such as AUKUS and the Global Combat Air Programme, with a target to become the largest defence exporter in Europe by 2030.|Maintain close ties with the United States, NATO, Five Eyes, the G7 and Indo-Pacific partners.|Return to spending 0.7% of GNI on development when fiscal circumstances allow, with a strict national interest test.
rows.sourceTitle: Conservative manifesto: Clear Plan. Bold Action. Secure Future.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a defence-led internationalist position that ties alliances and development spending to national security and fiscal conditions.

rows.headline: Fair immigration and asylum reform
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would end the Hostile Environment, scrap the Illegal Migration Act and Rwanda scheme, uphold the Refugee Convention, create safe and legal routes for refugees, process most asylum claims within three months, lift the work ban for asylum seekers waiting more than three months, and protect EU citizens' rights in the UK.
rows.detailedSummary: End the Hostile Environment and invest in officers, training and technology to tackle smuggling, trafficking and modern slavery.|Transfer work visa and overseas student policy-making out of the Home Office and into other departments.|Scrap the Illegal Migration Act and Rwanda scheme while upholding the Refugee Convention.|Establish a dedicated unit to improve asylum decisions, with a three-month service standard for all but the most complex claims, and speed up returns for people with no right to stay.|Lift the ban on asylum seekers working if they have waited more than three months for a decision.|Expand safe and legal routes, including humanitarian travel permits, refugee resettlement, child refugee resettlement, family reunion and community sponsorship.|Automatically grant full Settled Status to people with Pre-Settled Status and provide physical proof of their right to stay.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a liberal migration position focused on asylum processing, rights protections and safe routes, paired with enforcement against smuggling and returns where there is no right to stay.

rows.headline: Fair taxes, Europe and industrial strategy
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would manage public finances responsibly, repair the UK-EU trading relationship, launch an industrial strategy, support small businesses, replace business rates with a Commercial Landowner Levy, raise revenue from banks, tech firms, capital gains reform and share buybacks, and prioritise raising the income tax personal allowance when public finances allow.
rows.detailedSummary: Manage public finances with care and responsibility while seeking stability and growth.|Repair the broken trading relationship with Europe and remove as many trade barriers as possible.|Launch an industrial strategy, re-establish the Industrial Strategy Council on a statutory footing and support local and regional growth partnerships.|Abolish business rates and replace them with a Commercial Landowner Levy to support high streets.|Raise revenue by reversing Conservative tax cuts for big banks, increasing the Digital Services Tax from 2% to 6%, reforming capital gains tax loopholes and introducing a 4% tax on FTSE-100 share buyback schemes.|Make the party's priority for future tax cuts raising the income tax personal allowance when public finances allow.|Expand the British Business Bank and support skills, apprenticeships and fairer treatment for self-employed people.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines fiscal caution with targeted tax rises on banks, large firms and wealth-related loopholes, plus a strong emphasis on Europe and industrial policy.

rows.headline: GP access, dentistry, mental health and care
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would give everyone the right to see a GP within seven days or within 24 hours if urgent, recruit 8,000 more GPs, guarantee urgent and emergency NHS dental access, improve early mental health support, boost cancer survival with a 62-day treatment guarantee, invest in public health, fix ambulance delays, and tackle social care through workforce and funding reform.
rows.detailedSummary: Give everyone the right to see a GP within seven days, or within 24 hours if urgent, supported by 8,000 more full-time equivalent GPs.|Guarantee access to an NHS dentist for everyone needing urgent and emergency care.|Improve early access to mental health services through walk-in hubs for children and young people, regular mental health check-ups and mental health support in schools.|Guarantee 100% of cancer patients start treatment within 62 days from urgent referral and recruit more cancer nurses.|Invest in public health so people can spend five more years of life in good health.|Fix ambulance handover delays by increasing staffed hospital beds and improving social care.|Create a social care workforce plan, a Royal College of Care Workers, a higher Carer's Minimum Wage and a cross-party commission on sustainable social care funding.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is an access-focused health offer centred on primary care, dentistry, mental health and social care as pressure valves for the NHS.

rows.headline: Social homes, renters and leasehold reform
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would increase housebuilding to 380,000 homes a year across the UK, including 150,000 social homes, immediately ban no-fault evictions, make three-year tenancies the default, create a national register of licensed landlords, give councils powers to end Right to Buy locally, abolish residential leaseholds and cap ground rents, and introduce a Rent to Own model for social housing.
rows.detailedSummary: Increase building of new homes to 380,000 a year across the UK, including 150,000 social homes a year.|Use new garden cities and community-led development of cities and towns to support housing supply.|Immediately ban no-fault evictions, make three-year tenancies the default and create a national register of licensed landlords.|Give local authorities, including National Park Authorities, powers to end Right to Buy in their areas.|Abolish residential leaseholds and cap ground rents to a nominal fee.|Remove dangerous cladding from all buildings while ensuring leaseholders do not pay for it.|Introduce a Rent to Own model for social housing where rent payments build an increasing stake and ownership after 30 years.|End rough sleeping in the next parliament through a cross-Whitehall plan, emergency accommodation duties and stronger homelessness support.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a supply-and-rights housing platform, pairing a large social-home target with stronger renter, leaseholder and homelessness protections.

rows.headline: School funding, mental health and lifelong skills
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would put a qualified mental health professional in every primary and secondary school, increase school and college funding per pupil above inflation every year, repair crumbling school buildings, introduce a Tutoring Guarantee for disadvantaged pupils, invest in early years, extend free school meals to all children in poverty, create Lifelong Skills Grants for adults and reform SEND provision.
rows.detailedSummary: Put a dedicated, qualified mental health professional in every primary and secondary school.|Increase school and college funding per pupil above inflation every year and invest in buildings and repairs.|Introduce a Tutoring Guarantee for every disadvantaged pupil who needs extra support.|Invest in early years by giving disadvantaged three- and four-year-olds five extra free hours a week and tripling the Early Years Pupil Premium to GBP 1,000 a year.|Extend free school meals to all children in poverty, with an ambition to extend them to all primary pupils when public finances allow.|Create Lifelong Skills Grants, initially GBP 5,000 for adults to spend on education and training, with an aim to increase them to GBP 10,000 when finances allow.|Tackle SEND provision by giving local authorities extra funding and creating a National Body for SEND to fund high-needs support.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This education offer links attainment, mental health, early years and lifelong learning, with a strong focus on disadvantaged pupils and SEND.

rows.headline: Safety net, carers and anti-poverty reform
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would remove the two-child limit and benefit cap, set a target to end deep poverty within a decade, protect the pensions triple lock, increase and expand Carer's Allowance, reduce the first Universal Credit wait to five days, scrap the bedroom tax, replace sanctions with an incentive-based back-to-work scheme, and make disability benefits work better for disabled people.
rows.detailedSummary: Tackle child poverty by removing the two-child limit and the benefit cap.|Set a target of ending deep poverty within a decade and establish an independent commission to recommend annual Universal Credit increases so support covers essentials.|Protect the pensions triple lock.|Increase Carer's Allowance, expand eligibility, introduce an earnings taper, reduce the hours threshold and extend support to carers in full-time education.|Reduce the first Universal Credit payment wait from five weeks to five days.|Scrap the bedroom tax and replace the sanctions regime with an incentive-based scheme to help people into work.|End the young parent penalty by restoring the full Universal Credit rate for all parents regardless of age.|Give disabled people and disabled people's organisations a stronger voice in benefit policy, bring Work Capability Assessments in-house and establish an Independent Living Taskforce.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is an anti-poverty welfare position with prominent carer support, fewer punitive sanctions and stronger disability-policy participation.

rows.headline: Net zero by 2045 and clean energy
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would cut greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2045 at the latest, meet the UK's Paris target of at least a 68% emissions cut by 2030, launch an emergency Home Energy Upgrade programme, drive a rooftop solar revolution, generate 90% of UK electricity from renewables by 2030, maintain the fracking ban, ban new coal mines and end fossil fuel subsidies while supporting a just transition.
rows.detailedSummary: Cut greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2045 at the latest.|Meet the UK's Paris Agreement commitment to reduce emissions by at least 68% from 1990 levels by 2030.|Launch an emergency Home Energy Upgrade programme with free insulation and heat pumps for low-income households and a central role for local authorities.|Drive a rooftop solar revolution with expanded incentives and a guaranteed fair price for electricity sold back to the grid.|Invest in renewable power so 90% of UK electricity is generated from renewables by 2030.|Create a Net Zero Delivery Authority and a Joint Climate Council of the Nations to coordinate action.|Maintain the ban on fracking, introduce a ban on new coal mines and implement the G7 pledge to end fossil fuel subsidies while supporting a just transition.|Restore climate leadership through 0.7% development spending, Paris Agreement leadership and cooperation with Europe on emissions trading.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a high-ambition climate position, combining a faster net-zero target with household upgrades, renewables and institutional delivery mechanisms.

rows.headline: Public transport, rail reform and EV transition
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would make it cheaper and easier to switch to electric vehicles, restore the 2030 zero-emission requirement for new cars and small vans, freeze rail fares, simplify ticketing, extend rail electrification, improve stations and disabled access, reopen smaller stations, support rural buses, keep the GBP 2 bus fare cap while fares are reviewed, and expand zero-emission buses.
rows.detailedSummary: Rapidly roll out more electric vehicle charging points, reintroduce the plug-in car grant and restore the requirement that every new car and small van sold from 2030 is zero-emission.|Freeze rail fares and simplify public transport ticketing.|Significantly extend rail electrification, improve stations, improve disabled access, reopen smaller stations and deliver Northern Powerhouse Rail.|Establish a new Railway Agency as a public body to join up the industry, put commuters first, hold train companies to account and reform fares.|Support rural bus services and alternatives such as on-demand services where conventional buses are not viable.|Maintain the GBP 2 bus fare cap while fares are reviewed and replace multiple funding streams with one integrated local authority fund for expanding bus services and zero-emission vehicles.|Extend half-fares on buses, trams and trains to 18-year-olds and work on a Young Person's Buscard for 19- to 25-year-olds.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This transport platform prioritises affordability, rail coordination, bus access and electric-vehicle transition rather than road expansion.

rows.headline: Proportional representation and democratic rights
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would introduce proportional representation by the Single Transferable Vote for MPs and English local councillors, scrap voter ID, give 16- and 17-year-olds the vote, extend civic participation to settled EU citizens and long-term residents with permanent rights, cap political donations, enshrine the Ministerial Code in law, reform the House of Lords with a democratic mandate, protect the Human Rights Act and uphold the ECHR.
rows.detailedSummary: Introduce proportional representation by the Single Transferable Vote for electing MPs and local councillors in England.|Scrap the Conservatives' voter ID scheme and give 16- and 17-year-olds the right to vote.|Extend full civic participation, including voting and standing for office, to EU citizens with settled status and to others who have lived in the UK for at least five years and have permanent rights to stay.|Cap donations to political parties and require stronger registration of underrepresented voters.|Enshrine the Ministerial Code in legislation and strengthen accountability for harassment and bullying in Westminster.|Reform the House of Lords with a proper democratic mandate.|Introduce a written constitution for a federal United Kingdom and transfer more powers away from Westminster and Whitehall.|Champion the Human Rights Act and uphold the UK's commitment to the European Convention on Human Rights.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a major constitutional reform package centred on electoral reform, wider participation, decentralisation and rights protection.

rows.headline: Europe, defence, development and human rights
rows.shortSummary: The Liberal Democrats say they would fix the UK's relationship with Europe, seek a comprehensive agreement to remove trade barriers and eventually seek to join the Single Market, support NATO and increase defence spending every year with an ambition of at least 2.5% of GDP, stand with Ukraine, restore development spending to 0.7% of national income, re-establish an independent development department, support an immediate bilateral ceasefire in Gaza and uphold international law and human rights.
rows.detailedSummary: Fix the UK's relationship with Europe through a staged roadmap, including partnerships with EU agencies and programmes, deeper trading arrangements and eventually seeking to join the Single Market.|Maintain support for NATO and increase defence spending every year of the parliament, with an ambition to spend at least 2.5% of GDP on defence.|Seek a defence and security agreement with the EU and strengthen cooperation with NATO, Five Eyes and European allies.|Stand with Ukraine and provide support against Russia's invasion, including work to repurpose frozen Russian assets for Ukraine.|Restore the UK's reputation as an international development superpower by returning spending to 0.7% of national income and re-establishing an independent development department.|Advocate for an immediate bilateral ceasefire in the Israel-Gaza conflict and a two-state solution based on 1967 borders, with immediate recognition of Palestine.|Protect human rights abroad through Magnitsky sanctions, import bans from areas with egregious abuses, consular rights for politically detained British nationals and support for LGBT+ rights and freedom of belief.
rows.sourceTitle: Liberal Democrat manifesto: For a Fair Deal
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a strongly internationalist position, with Europe, development spending, NATO, Ukraine and rights-based diplomacy all treated as core foreign-policy pillars.

rows.headline: Healthcare and NHS access
rows.shortSummary: Restore Britain says the NHS should be restored as a national health service serving British people first. Its healthcare pledges include free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors, removing medical school caps, and prioritising British-trained doctors.
rows.detailedSummary: Restore the NHS as a national health service serving British people first.|Offer free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors.|Remove medical school caps and prioritise British-trained doctors.
rows.sourceTitle: Restore Britain healthcare policy
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames NHS pressure mainly through national access, workforce and migration rather than through private-sector reform.
